topic: admissions overview; required materials; no application fee
text:
Heinz College Admissions

This section applies to Heinz College's master's and Ph.D. admissions only. Heinz College does not charge an application fee.

How to Apply: Full-time Master's Programs. Course prerequisites and required materials vary by program, but all applicants to full-time Master's programs at Heinz College must submit the following: an online application, transcripts, letters of recommendation (2 required, 3 maximum), a professional resume, and a required essay. There is no application fee to apply to Heinz College.

topic: admissions overview; required materials; no application fee
text:
There is no application fee to apply to Heinz College. International applicants may be required to also submit English Language Proficiency Exam Results and Transcript Evaluations. All applicants can also submit an optional recommended Video Interview and an optional essay.

Full-time master's programs at Heinz College include: Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence Systems Management (AIM), Master of Arts Management (MAM), Master of Entertainment Industry Management (MEIM), Master of Science in Health Care Analytics (MSHCA), Master of Information Systems Management (MISM, including the BIDA pathway), Master of Science in Information Security Policy and Management (MSISPM), and Master of Science in Public Policy and Management (MSPPM).

Part-time master's programs include: Master of Public Management (MPM), Master of Medical Management (MMM), and Master of Science in Information Technology (MSIT).

Partner Scholarships: If you are affiliated with one of Heinz's partner organizations such as Peace Corps, AmeriCorps, Teach For America or City Year, you are eligible to receive a minimum scholarship of $8,100 or $13,000 per semester.

Contact: Heinz College Office of Admissions, 5000 Forbes Avenue, Hamburg Hall, Pittsburgh, PA 15213-3890. Phone: 412-268-2164. Email: hnzadmit@andrew.cmu.edu.

text:
Admissions FAQs — Application Process

When can I apply? The application for admission opens September 1 for the following calendar year (for example, September 1, 2025 for the January, May, and August 2026 intakes).

I am currently pursuing my bachelor's degree. Can I still apply by the Round 1 deadline? Applicants who are currently pursuing their bachelor's degree are encouraged to apply by the Round 2 deadline (January 10) so their fall (or seventh) semester grades are included on the official transcript/evaluation report.

topic: application process; deadlines; GRE/GMAT; TOEFL; recommendations; transcripts
text:
y by the Round 2 deadline (January 10) so their fall (or seventh) semester grades are included on the official transcript/evaluation report. What do I need to submit for admissions consideration? Required application materials vary by program and applicant type (domestic or international). In general, to apply for admission to a master's program, prospective students are required to submit an online application form, undergraduate transcripts, their professional resume, two recommendations, and an essay.

What is the application fee? There is no application fee to apply to Heinz College.

topic: application process; deadlines; GRE/GMAT; TOEFL; recommendations; transcripts
text:
What is the application fee? There is no application fee to apply to Heinz College. Can I apply to more than one program? Heinz strongly recommends applicants apply to one program. Students interested in more than one program are encouraged to apply to their top choice and can indicate interest in a second program within their optional essay.

Am I allowed to use ChatGPT or other AI tools to write my essay? You should not use artificial intelligence such as ChatGPT to write any portion of your essay. Your essay should contain only true information and your own original content. Submission of false, plagiarized, or AI-generated material may result in automatic denial of admission.

topic: application process; deadlines; GRE/GMAT; TOEFL; recommendations; transcripts
text: Submission of false, plagiarized, or AI-generated material may result in automatic denial of admission. Can I still apply if I missed the application deadline? Heinz does accept late applications to its master's programs. For full-time master's programs, applications are accepted after the published deadlines if space remains in the incoming class; contact the Office of Admissions if interested in applying after January 10. On June 15, 2026, the application system will close for admission to the full-time master's programs' incoming cohort for August 2026. PhD programs do not accept applications after the December 1 deadline.

topic: application process; deadlines; GRE/GMAT; TOEFL; recommendations; transcripts
text:
PhD programs do not accept applications after the December 1 deadline. Can I defer my admission? Deferrals are not common and are considered on a case-by-case basis for one academic year only. Typically deferrals are granted for military/volunteer public service or opportunities with a partner organization.

Admissions FAQs — GRE/GMAT

Are GRE/GMAT scores required? The GRE/GMAT is required for admission to most of the master's and Ph.D. programs offered by Heinz College. Each program has its own policy. In some cases, applicants may qualify for a waiver of the test score requirement and apply without test scores.

topic: application process; deadlines; GRE/GMAT; TOEFL; recommendations; transcripts
text:
In some cases, applicants may qualify for a waiver of the test score requirement and apply without test scores. What are Heinz College's GRE codes? The institution code is 3085. Scores sent to the main Carnegie Mellon University code (2074) will still be verified.

How old can GRE test scores be? Heinz accepts test scores that are up to five years old at the time of application. ETS will not verify test scores beyond the five year mark.

Can I take the GRE/GMAT after the application deadline? On the application, enter your upcoming test date and registration number in lieu of your scores, and still submit your application prior to the posted deadline.

Admissions FAQs — TOEFL/IELTS

topic: application process; deadlines; GRE/GMAT; TOEFL; recommendations; transcripts
text:
Admissions FAQs — TOEFL/IELTS Do I need to take the TOEFL exam? If your native language is not English, you are required to submit an official score report from TOEFL, IELTS, or Duolingo. Exceptions apply for applicants who have worked or studied for five or more years in specified English-speaking countries, or who studied four years of their undergraduate education in the United States and earned their bachelor's degree from a regionally accredited US school. Applicants must score at or above the posted minimums. Heinz's TOEFL institution code is 2176. TOEFL scores are accepted up to two years old.

Admissions FAQs — Recommendations

topic: application process; deadlines; GRE/GMAT; TOEFL; recommendations; transcripts
text:
Admissions FAQs — Recommendations How many recommendations are required? At least two recommendations are required to support an application; you may exclude or change recommenders in the online application. Under FERPA, students may waive their right to view letters of recommendation; how you respond to the FERPA waiver question will not impact your chances for admission.

Admissions FAQs — Transcripts and GPA

topic: application process; deadlines; GRE/GMAT; TOEFL; recommendations; transcripts
text:
Admissions FAQs — Transcripts and GPA Is my GPA good enough for admission? Heinz College does not have a required minimum GPA. The average and median GPA for admitted applicants are available on each program page. The Admissions Committee completes a holistic review of each application, considering transcripts, test scores, recommendations, personal statement, and career goals.

Do international transcripts need third-party evaluation? All international academic documents for applicants to master's programs (except Master of Medical Management) must be verified by a transcript evaluation service.

Frequently Asked Questions — General and Curriculum

About the name. Heinz College was originally the School of Urban and Public Affairs (SUPA). It was renamed the H. John Heinz III School of Public Policy and Management in 1992 after Pennsylvania Senator H. John Heinz III, and became Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy in 2008. The Master of Information Systems Management program was established in 1996.

How do I apply? Applications must be submitted through Heinz College's online application system; Heinz only accepts applications online. Full-time master's and Ph.D. students must matriculate in the Fall semester. There is no application fee.

Full-time master's and Ph.D. students must matriculate in the Fall semester. There is no application fee. Part-time programs. For working professionals with at least three years of work experience, Heinz offers two part-time programs — the Master of Public Management (MPM) and the Master of Science in Information Technology (MSIT) — with evening classes and remote learning options. These part-time programs have additional start dates throughout the year.

Deferrals. Deferrals are not common and are considered case-by-case for one academic year only, typically for military/volunteer public service or opportunities with a partner organization.

dered case-by-case for one academic year only, typically for military/volunteer public service or opportunities with a partner organization. STEM designation. Several Heinz master's programs are STEM-designated (eligible for the STEM OPT extension), including AIM, MISM (and the BIDA pathway), MSISPM, MSHCA, and MSPPM. Master of Arts Management, Master of Entertainment Industry Management, Master of Public Management, Master of Medical Management, and the online MSIT are not STEM-designated.

Capstone instead of thesis. In lieu of a master's thesis, full-time master's students complete a team-based Capstone Project in collaboration with a real-world client.

In lieu of a master's thesis, full-time master's students complete a team-based Capstone Project in collaboration with a real-world client. What is a unit? At Carnegie Mellon, courses are counted in units rather than credits. A 12-unit course is roughly equivalent to a 4-credit-hour course elsewhere. Most Heinz courses are 12 units (a full-semester course) or 6 units (a half-semester "mini" course). The academic year is divided into four minis (two per semester), with summer minis 5 and 6 for students taking summer classes.

The academic year is divided into four minis (two per semester), with summer minis 5 and 6 for students taking summer classes. Transfer credits. Heinz does not accept transfer credits from institutions outside Carnegie Mellon toward its full-time degree programs. Graduate-level coursework completed in another CMU program may be evaluated case-by-case after admission. Part-time MPM and MSIT students may request evaluation of prior graduate coursework for up to 24 transfer units after accepting admission. Heinz also offers exemption exams during onboarding for students with overlapping prior coursework.

Financial Aid FAQs

Tuition rate. Details on the cost of attendance for Heinz College programs are published on the CMU Student Financial Services graduate tuition website. Heinz College does not offer tuition waivers to master's students.

Heinz College does not offer tuition waivers to master's students. How do I apply for scholarships? The Heinz College Office of Admissions gives scholarship consideration to every admitted student who indicates, within the application for admission, that they want to be considered for merit-based scholarships. The Admissions Committee reviews the information in the application to determine each student's scholarship award. No separate scholarship application is needed unless otherwise stated in the scholarship's description. Master's students do not need to submit a financial aid application to be considered for scholarships and fellowships; these awards are made at the time of admission.

eed to submit a financial aid application to be considered for scholarships and fellowships; these awards are made at the time of admission. When are scholarships posted? Scholarships are posted to student accounts prior to the start of the semester for registered students and are adjusted as tuition charges change.

External scholarships. Applicants may research additional funding through resources such as CMU's Fellowships and Scholarships Office (FSO), CMU Student Financial Services external scholarship listings, and third-party scholarship search sites.

ships and Scholarships Office (FSO), CMU Student Financial Services external scholarship listings, and third-party scholarship search sites. Veterans education benefits. Heinz College participates in the G.I. Bill and the Yellow Ribbon program. Eligibility for VA benefits is determined by the Department of Veterans Affairs; students provide their Certificate of Eligibility to CMU's VA Certifying Official. VA education benefits are not taxable.

Federal Work Study. Work study is a federal aid program allowing eligible students to earn money toward education expenses. To be considered, a student must be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident, be eligible for federal financial aid, and submit the FAFSA each year. Work study eligibility is indicated in the student's financial aid offer letter.

Work study eligibility is indicated in the student's financial aid offer letter. Financial wellness. CMU provides financial wellness and debt-management resources through Student Financial Services and the Financial Wellness at CMU website.

Application Checklist — Transcripts

All applicants who have completed (or will complete) their bachelor's degree in the United States must have an official transcript of their undergraduate studies submitted directly to Carnegie Mellon University's Heinz College by their undergraduate university.

ranscript of their undergraduate studies submitted directly to Carnegie Mellon University's Heinz College by their undergraduate university. All applicants who have completed (or will complete) their bachelor's degree outside the United States must have a course-by-course transcript evaluation report submitted directly to Heinz College via an approved transcript evaluation service. All international academic documents for applicants to master's programs (except the Master of Medical Management) must be verified by a transcript evaluation service.

documents for applicants to master's programs (except the Master of Medical Management) must be verified by a transcript evaluation service. All applicants must earn a bachelor's degree from a regionally accredited institution (or its equivalent) prior to the date they would begin their studies at Heinz College. Applicants who have not completed their bachelor's degree at the time of application may be admitted on the condition that they submit their final, official transcript, including the date of graduation, prior to the start of their first semester.

Heinz College does not have a required minimum GPA. The Admissions Committee completes a holistic review of each application, considering transcripts, test scores, recommendations, personal statement, and career goals.

Application Checklist — Recommendations

You are required to have two recommendation forms submitted on your behalf. Recommendations should be provided by employers, supervisors, professors, or others who can comment on your professional and educational goals and potential. At least one of your recommenders should know you in a professional setting (full-time employment, internship, part-time employment, research, or non-academic extracurricular activity). You may opt to have up to three recommendations submitted in support of your application.

You may opt to have up to three recommendations submitted in support of your application. Recommendations from friends, family members, acquaintances, and other sources not able to evaluate applicants on an academic or professional basis will not be accepted. Letters of recommendation must be written in or translated into English, and must be submitted electronically through the online application system by your recommenders. Recommendations submitted on paper are not accepted.

You may change or exclude recommenders in the online application. Under the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA), students may waive their right to view letters of recommendation; how you respond to the FERPA waiver question does not affect your chances for admission.

Application Checklist — Video Interview

A short video interview is optional but strongly recommended. It is especially important for applicants who are unable to visit campus prior to the application deadline or who are non-native English speakers.

You will receive an email invitation to complete the video interview after you submit your online application and after Heinz College receives your official undergraduate transcript (or course-by-course evaluation report).

topic: English proficiency exam (TOEFL/IELTS/Duolingo) requirements and minimums
text:
Application Checklist — English Language Proficiency Exam Results

If your native language (mother tongue) is not English, you are required to submit an official score report from the TOEFL, IELTS, or Duolingo English Test. The only exceptions are for applicants who have worked or studied for five or more years in a specified list of English-speaking countries (including Australia, Canada, Ireland, New Zealand, Singapore, South Africa, the United Kingdom, and the United States), or who studied for four years of their undergraduate education in the United States and earned their bachelor's degree from a regionally accredited US school.

topic: English proficiency exam (TOEFL/IELTS/Duolingo) requirements and minimums
text: four years of their undergraduate education in the United States and earned their bachelor's degree from a regionally accredited US school. Applicants required to submit scores must have them reported directly to Heinz College by the testing service, and must score at or above the posted minimums. Heinz College's TOEFL institution code is 2176. TOEFL and IELTS/Duolingo scores are accepted up to two years old. Applicants may submit scores from up to four valid exam dates, and Heinz College uses the highest verified score in each section. When required, applicants are strongly encouraged to take the exam at least one month prior to the application deadline.

text:
Connect With Us — Heinz College Events for Prospective Students

Prospective students can connect with the Heinz College admissions office, program directors, and current students on campus, on the road, or online. An events calendar lets prospective students find and register for events; events are color-coded (on-campus in red, off-campus in green, Washington, D.C. events in teal, online events in blue, and student-led events in yellow).

topic: partner scholarships (Peace Corps, AmeriCorps, TFA, City Year)
text:
Strategic Partner Scholarship Program

Heinz College partners with organizations and institutions to provide financial support that reduces the cost of graduate school. No separate application is necessary — to be considered, indicate your experience, involvement, membership, or affiliation with a partner on your application for admission to Heinz College. If you do not list the partner on your application, you will not be eligible for the award later. Affiliation with more than one partner does not increase the total available scholarship award.

topic: partner scholarships (Peace Corps, AmeriCorps, TFA, City Year)
text: Affiliation with more than one partner does not increase the total available scholarship award. Foundational Partners. Applicants affiliated with a Foundational Partner who enroll in a full-time Heinz graduate program are eligible for a minimum scholarship equal to 50% and up to 100% of tuition. Foundational Partners include AmeriCorps VISTA, City Year, the Peace Corps Paul D. Coverdell Fellowship, Teach For America, the Fulbright Scholar Program, the Coro Fellowship, the Congressional Hunger Center, and the Chicano Latino Youth Leadership Project (Comcast Fellows).

topic: list of Heinz master's programs; differences among programs
text:
Heinz College Programs (Index)

Heinz College offers master's, doctoral, bachelor's, and certificate programs across two schools (the School of Information Systems & Management and the School of Public Policy & Management) plus College of Fine Arts joint-degree programs.

topic: list of Heinz master's programs; differences among programs
text: he School of Information Systems & Management and the School of Public Policy & Management) plus College of Fine Arts joint-degree programs. Full-time master's programs: Master of Science in Public Policy and Management (MSPPM), including the Data Analytics pathway (MSPPM-DA) and the Washington, D.C. pathway (MSPPM-DC); Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence Systems Management (AIM); Master of Information Systems Management (MISM), including the Business Intelligence & Data Analytics pathway (BIDA); Master of Arts Management (MAM); Master of Science in Information Security Policy & Management (MSISPM); Master of Entertainment Industry Management (MEIM); and Master of Science in Health Care Analytics & Information Technology (MSHCA).

SISPM); Master of Entertainment Industry Management (MEIM); and Master of Science in Health Care Analytics & Information Technology (MSHCA). Part-time master's programs: Master of Medical Management for Physicians (MMM); Master of Public Management (MPM); and the online Master of Science in Information Technology (MSIT), which includes IT Management, Business Intelligence & Data Analytics, and Information Security & Assurance tracks.

Doctoral programs: Ph.D. in Public Policy & Management and Ph.D. in Information Systems & Management.

Undergraduate: Bachelor of Science in Information Systems.

Certificate and executive-education programs are also offered, including Managing AI Systems and several chief-officer certificates.

topic: list of Heinz master's programs; differences among programs
text: Certificate and executive-education programs are also offered, including Managing AI Systems and several chief-officer certificates. Prospective applicants can filter programs by frequency (full-time or part-time), degree type, area of interest (the arts, entertainment, data analytics, technology, health care, public policy, cybersecurity, smart cities), years of work experience, programming experience, and location (Pittsburgh, Los Angeles, Washington, D.C., or online/hybrid).

2026-2027 Heinz College Graduate Tuition and Fees

These are the 2026-2027 tuition and fee rates for Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy graduate programs. Tuition is set annually by Carnegie Mellon University Student Financial Services and is subject to change each academic year.

Master of Arts Management (MAM): Tuition $29,570 per semester; Fees $518 per semester.

Heinz College Ph.D. Programs: Tuition $29,570 per semester; Fees $518 per semester.

Master of Entertainment Industry Management (MEIM): Tuition $29,570 per semester; Fees vary.

Master of Information Systems Management (MISM): Tuition varies (charged by unit); Fees vary.

Master of Information Systems Management (MISM): Tuition varies (charged by unit); Fees vary. Master of Medical Management (MMM): Tuition varies; Fees vary.

Master of Public Management (MPM): Tuition $620 per unit; Fees $518 per semester.

Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence Systems Management (AIM): Tuition varies; Fees $518 per semester.

Master of Science in Health Care Analytics and Information Technology (MSHCA): Tuition $29,570 per semester; Fees $518 per semester.

Master of Science in Information Security Policy and Management (MSISPM): Tuition $29,570 per semester; Fees $518 per semester.

Master of Science in Information Technology (MSIT): Tuition $620 per unit; Fees vary.

Master of Science in Information Technology (MSIT): Tuition $620 per unit; Fees vary. Master of Science in Public Policy and Management (MSPPM): Tuition $29,570 per semester; Fees vary.

For programs listed as "varies," exact tuition depends on the number of units enrolled; refer to the program-specific Student Financial Services tuition page for details.

Cost of Attendance (CMU Student Financial Services)

Cost of Attendance is an estimate of expenses while enrolled at Carnegie Mellon University. It includes direct costs such as tuition, fees, housing, and meals, as well as indirect costs such as books, transportation, and personal expenses. Indirect costs are not billed by the university, but both direct and indirect costs are considered when CMU determines financial aid offers.

Indirect costs are not billed by the university, but both direct and indirect costs are considered when CMU determines financial aid offers. Graduate tuition rates are set by each college or school (undergraduate tuition is set by the Board of Trustees and university leadership). Current tuition and fee schedules are published for the 2026-2027 and 2025-2026 academic years, with separate graduate and undergraduate pages, plus rates for visiting/non-degree students and summer sessions.

Questions about costs: prospective students can contact the Office of Admission at admission@andrew.cmu.edu; enrolled students can contact The HUB at thehub@andrew.cmu.edu.

Affording Your AI Master's Degree (AIM) — Scholarships and Student Finances

Many AIM students receive a scholarship from Heinz College to reduce the cost of their degree. Applicants should indicate on the application for admission that they wish to be considered for merit-based support.

AI Systems Management Program Scholarships. Merit-based scholarships awarded on the basis of the application for admission, ranging from 10% to 75% of tuition. Every admitted student is considered; no additional application or documentation is required. Recipients may also earn $5,000–$6,000 per academic year through a research assistantship or part-time work for non-tuition expenses.

Recipients may also earn $5,000–$6,000 per academic year through a research assistantship or part-time work for non-tuition expenses. Other AIM scholarships and fellowships. Excellence in Technology Fellowships (a limited number of half-tuition awards); American Technology Fellowships (minimum $10,000 per semester up to full tuition, for U.S. citizens committed to IT management); the David Lingren Fellowship for Technology and Society (an additional $2,000 per semester plus a research assistantship with the Block Center); the Student Experience & Engagement (SEE) Fellowship (up to 50% tuition plus an RA role); the Deliberative Discourse Fellowship (an additional $2,000 per semester); and Pittsburgh Regional Leaders Scholarships. Some fellowships require an additional application, offered to students admitted by the January 10 deadline.

Some fellowships require an additional application, offered to students admitted by the January 10 deadline. Partnership and education-partner scholarships. Applicants affiliated with a strategic partner organization, or who completed a bachelor's degree at a regional/national education partner, are eligible for partnership-based scholarships (education partners: 30% up to 100% of tuition). Indicate the affiliation on the application for admission.

Veterans. U.S. military veterans not receiving 100% tuition benefits through the VA are eligible for a scholarship of up to 30% of tuition, also available to spouses and children of veterans. Heinz also has a Fallen Heroes Scholarship Program.

Heinz also has a Fallen Heroes Scholarship Program. Student employment and loans. Students may be eligible for Federal Work Study, Federal Community Service Work Study, or non-federal work study positions (typically $17/hour; students can access up to $18,000 in work-study awards). Federal direct loans and private education loans are available through CMU Student Financial Services.

Federal direct loans and private education loans are available through CMU Student Financial Services. General scholarship terms. Heinz scholarships and fellowships are typically renewable for subsequent semesters but are not guaranteed, and may be reduced or rescinded for reasons such as changing the program of study, enrolling in fewer units, extending program length, deferring admission, failing to make satisfactory academic progress, or submitting inaccurate application information. Total scholarship from all sources generally may not exceed tuition and fees. Estimated cost of attendance for AIM students is published on the CMU Student Financial Services website.

CMU Funding Opportunities and Fellowships (Office of Graduate and Postdoctoral Affairs)

The Office of Graduate and Postdoctoral Affairs administers some of the financial support available to graduate students, and there are several fellowship and scholarship opportunities for prospective graduate students. Information on tuition and fees is on the Student Financial Services website; prospective students should also review the Prospective Graduate Student Information page and consult their department and advisor for additional opportunities.

should also review the Prospective Graduate Student Information page and consult their department and advisor for additional opportunities. Funding support for current graduate students includes Conference Funding (to present at conferences in their field), Professional Engagement Funding (for conferences and other professional opportunities), and Scholarly Project Funding (small grants of $750 toward research required for a graduate degree). Stipend details vary by program and should be confirmed with the department.

Stipend details vary by program and should be confirmed with the department. Fellowship opportunities for prospective graduate students include the CMU Rales Fellows Program, which is dedicated to cultivating the next generation of STEM leaders by increasing access to graduate education. Additional resources include CMU's Fellowships and Scholarships Office (FSO), student emergency support funding, and higher-education tax benefit information.

topic: university-wide graduate admissions policy and financial support
text:
Carnegie Mellon University — Prospective Graduate Student Information (Office of Graduate and Postdoctoral Affairs)

Carnegie Mellon University is home to master's and doctoral programs within its seven schools and colleges, and also offers online programs and interdisciplinary programs.

topic: university-wide graduate admissions policy and financial support
text: ome to master's and doctoral programs within its seven schools and colleges, and also offers online programs and interdisciplinary programs. Admission and application process. Each program manages its own separate admissions process, including deadlines and requirements, and this information varies by department. Prospective students are encouraged to visit the department's website for specific details. A student's qualifications are not assessed until a full application has been received and processed. Applicants are welcome to apply to more than one program, but in most cases must complete separate applications for each. All admissions-related questions — including where to submit transcripts, exam requirements, and how to set up a visit — should be directed to the department in which the applicant is interested.

topic: university-wide graduate admissions policy and financial support
text: ubmit transcripts, exam requirements, and how to set up a visit — should be directed to the department in which the applicant is interested. Tuition and financial support. For the most part, students in the master's programs are self-supporting, and Ph.D. students are at least partially funded. Each program makes its own arrangements regarding financial support of potential graduate students. Applicants should seek information from the department of interest for their specific opportunities. More information regarding tuition and fees is available on the Student Financial Services website, and centralized fellowship and funding resources are available through the Funding Opportunities and Fellowships page.

topic: term start/end dates and academic calendar
text:
CMU Academic Calendar (The HUB)

The Academic Calendar is Carnegie Mellon University's information source and planning document for important dates. It includes registration dates, class start dates, add/drop deadlines, exam dates, and more.

Doctoral course-sections follow a separate academic calendar, and Heinz College maintains its own academic calendar (distinct from the university-wide calendar). Separate calendars also exist for the Tepper School of Business graduate courses, CMU-Africa, and CMU-Qatar.

topic: term start/end dates and academic calendar
text: Separate calendars also exist for the Tepper School of Business graduate courses, CMU-Africa, and CMU-Qatar. Full academic calendars are published per academic year as downloadable documents (for example, the 2025-2026 and 2026-2027 academic years), including iCal and Google Calendar formats and Pittsburgh class-meeting-day schedules. Specific term start dates and deadlines are listed in those linked calendar documents. Questions can be directed to the University Registrar's Office at cmuregistrar@andrew.cmu.edu.

text:
Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence Systems Management (AIM) — Program Overview

Key program information. Program length: 16 months, 3 semesters plus internship (AIM with internship), or 12 months, 3 semesters (fast track). Application deadlines: December 1 (Round 1), January 10 (Round 2), February 2 (Round 3). Degree earned: Master of Science. Internship: required for AIM with internship, not required for the AIM accelerated (fast track) option. STEM-designated: Yes.

topic: program format, duration, deadlines, degree, STEM
text:
Internship: required for AIM with internship, not required for the AIM accelerated (fast track) option. STEM-designated: Yes. The Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence Systems Management (AIM) program is housed in Carnegie Mellon University's Heinz College and teaches students to put AI to work for businesses and stakeholders. Every AIM student completes a capstone project, engaging directly with Heinz College's network of industry collaborators.

In the 16-month program with required internship, students work alongside industry leaders and complete an internship. In the fast-track 12-month AIM program, an internship is not required. The AIM degree is a STEM-designated degree program.

Master of Information Systems Management (MISM) — Program Overview

Key program information. Program length: 16 months, 3 semesters plus internship. Application deadlines: December 1 (Round 1), January 10 (Round 2), February 2 (Round 3). Degree earned: Master's degree. Internship: required (a waiver can be requested based on three-plus years of prior relevant full-time work experience). STEM-designated: Yes.

STEM-designated: Yes. The Master of Information Systems Management (MISM) program blends technical and leadership skills, with coursework in data analysis and hands-on learning through internships and capstone projects with industry clients. MISM offers two academic tracks: the Information Systems Traditional Track (16-month) and the Business Intelligence and Data Analytics (BIDA) pathway, along with optional concentrations. Heinz College is the #1 ranked Information Systems program.

Master of Science in Public Policy and Management (MSPPM) — Program Overview

Key program information. Program length: 2 years, 4 semesters (a 3-semester track is available for some students). Application deadlines: December 1 (Round 1), January 10 (Round 2), February 2 (Round 3). Degree earned: Master of Science. Internship: required (except the 3-semester track). STEM-designated: Yes.

Degree earned: Master of Science. Internship: required (except the 3-semester track). STEM-designated: Yes. The Master of Science in Public Policy and Management (MSPPM) program combines policy curriculum with instruction in statistics, economics, finance, organizational design, and presentation skills. MSPPM offers several tracks: the Flagship (Pittsburgh two-year) track, the Data Analytics track, the Washington, D.C. track, and the Fast Track (three-semester), along with policy concentrations and dual-degree options. Heinz College's Public Policy and Management program is ranked No. 8 in the United States by U.S. News & World Report.

Master of Science in Information Security Policy and Management (MSISPM) — Program Overview

Key program information. Program length: 2 years, 4 semesters. Application deadlines: December 1 (Round 1), January 10 (Round 2), February 2 (Round 3). Degree earned: Master of Science. Internship: required. STEM-designated: Yes.

Degree earned: Master of Science. Internship: required. STEM-designated: Yes. The Master of Science in Information Security Policy and Management (MSISPM) program is a cybersecurity master's covering digital security, physical security, insider threat, network defenses, data analytics, and management. It is taught by Heinz College faculty and practitioners from the CERT Division of CMU's Software Engineering Institute. Carnegie Mellon is designated by the NSA and Department of Homeland Security as a Center of Academic Excellence in Cyber Defense, Cyber Operations, and Research.

Master of Science in Health Care Analytics and Information Technology (MSHCA) — Program Overview

Key program information. Program length: 2 years, 4 semesters. Application deadlines: December 1 (Round 1), January 10 (Round 2), February 2 (Round 3). Degree earned: Master of Science. Internship: required. STEM-designated: Yes.

Degree earned: Master of Science. Internship: required. STEM-designated: Yes. The Master of Science in Health Care Analytics and Information Technology (MSHCA) program is an interdisciplinary program blending health care, information technology, and data analytics to improve patient outcomes and streamline health care operations. Students learn to manage and analyze health data, implement health information systems, and lead projects that improve health care delivery, preparing for roles in hospitals, research institutions, public health organizations, and the health care technology industry. Graduates earn an average starting salary of about $105,000.

Master of Arts Management (MAM) — Program Overview

Key program information. Program length: 2 years, 4 semesters. Application deadlines: December 1 (Round 1), January 10 (Round 2), February 2 (Round 3). Degree earned: Master's degree. Internship: required. STEM-designated: No.

Degree earned: Master's degree. Internship: required. STEM-designated: No. The Master of Arts Management (MAM) program is a joint degree program with Carnegie Mellon University's College of Fine Arts. It centers on the intersection of people, policy, and technology with an arts focus, combining courses in analytics and quantitative management with industry-centered experiential learning to prepare leaders for arts and cultural institutions. MAM students take classes with faculty from both Heinz College and the College of Fine Arts.

Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence Systems Management (AIM) — Application Process

Application deadlines. Round 1 deadline: December 1, 2026. Round 2 deadline: January 10, 2027. Round 3 deadline: February 1, 2027. Applicants for all three rounds of admission are given equal consideration for merit-based scholarships, competitive scholarships, and fellowship awards.

r all three rounds of admission are given equal consideration for merit-based scholarships, competitive scholarships, and fellowship awards. Admission decision timelines. Students who submit their application and supporting materials by December 1 (Round 1) receive their admission decision by the end of January. Round 2 (January 10) decisions are released by the middle of March. Round 3 (February 1) decisions are released by the end of March. Round 3 is the latest deadline for full consideration for competitive scholarship and fellowship awards.

Round 3 is the latest deadline for full consideration for competitive scholarship and fellowship awards. Applying after the deadlines. Heinz continues to accept applications after the published deadlines if space remains in the incoming class; contact the Office of Admissions to apply after February 1. Late applicants receive a decision approximately four weeks after their complete application is received and processed. On June 15, 2027, the application system closes for the full-time master's incoming cohort for August 2027.

On June 15, 2027, the application system closes for the full-time master's incoming cohort for August 2027. Required application materials. A completed online application (there is no application fee); official transcripts (US applicants) or a course-by-course transcript evaluation report (international applicants); a professional one- to two-page résumé; two recommendations (up to three allowed, at least one professional, submitted electronically in English); and the required essay. All applicants must earn a bachelor's degree from a regionally accredited institution before beginning studies; applicants who have not finished may be admitted on the condition that they submit a final official transcript before their first semester.

applicants who have not finished may be admitted on the condition that they submit a final official transcript before their first semester. Required essay. The essay should address three questions — career goals, prior experience, and why Heinz College — with a recommended length of 700 words minimum (up to about 1,100 words). Applicants may not use AI tools such as ChatGPT to write any portion of the essay. An optional essay of up to 250 words may address gaps or weaknesses. A short video interview is optional but strongly recommended.

An optional essay of up to 250 words may address gaps or weaknesses. A short video interview is optional but strongly recommended. GRE/GMAT policy. GRE/GMAT scores are required, but applicants can qualify for a waiver by submitting one or more of: (1) evidence of prior quantitative coursework — a grade of B or higher in a college-level, credit-bearing Pre-Calculus class or higher (AP or IB Precalculus/Calculus counts if it appears as college credit on the transcript); or (2) an undergraduate degree from Carnegie Mellon University. Approval of a waiver is at the discretion of the admissions committee. Prospective AIM students are not eligible for a GRE/GMAT waiver based on work experience.

Prospective AIM students are not eligible for a GRE/GMAT waiver based on work experience. Prerequisite quantitative and technical preparation. The AIM program is quantitatively focused. Before starting classes, students must have completed college/university-level coursework in: (1) Pre-Calculus (a calculus class or higher also satisfies this), (2) Introductory Statistics, and (3) Introductory Python programming (Java and C++ are also acceptable). Applicants who do not meet all three at the time of application may be offered conditional admission and required to complete the missing courses over the summer before their first semester.

f application may be offered conditional admission and required to complete the missing courses over the summer before their first semester. Courses must be college-level at an accredited institution, for credit, and completed with a grade of B or higher; they may be taken on campus or online. Courses from Coursera or other MOOC platforms do not meet the prerequisite requirement. Admitted students who have not completed a programming course must take and pass a free online "Introduction to Python" course offered by Heinz College the summer before enrolling.

The Master of Information Systems Management program has two academic tracks emphasizing multidisciplinary thinking, teamwork, and leadership through experiential learning and co-curricular programming. Some pathways have different application requirements, so applicants should review the application deadline and requirements before beginning the online application. The MISM program is a STEM-designated degree program.

Applicants for all three rounds of admission are given equal consideration for merit-based scholarships, competitive scholarships, and fellowship awards.

Round 1 (December 1) is best for applicants who want the earliest decision (end of January).

Students should submit their application form and the required supporting materials by the listed deadline date: essay, resume, two recommendations, official transcript or evaluation report for the bachelor's degree, and official test scores (if applicable). The Admissions Committee completes a holistic review of each application.

The essay, résumé, two recommendations, an official transcript or evaluation report for the bachelor's degree, and official test scores (if applicable). A completed online application is required and there is no application fee.

A completed online application is required and there is no application fee. GRE/GMAT policy. GRE/GMAT scores are required, but applicants may qualify for a waiver by submitting one or more of: (1) evidence of prior quantitative coursework — a grade of B or higher in a college-level, credit-bearing Pre-Calculus class or higher; (2) evidence of quantitative work experience with a significant analytical component, documented and confirmed by a supervisor recommender; or (3) an undergraduate degree from Carnegie Mellon University. Unlike some Heinz programs, MSHCA does allow a waiver based on quantitative work experience. Applicants who have taken the MCAT may share those scores. Before starting classes, students must have completed college-level coursework in: (1) Pre-Calculus (a calculus class or higher also satisfies this), (2) Introductory Statistics, and (3) Introductory Python programming (Java and C++ are also acceptable). Applicants who do not meet all three may be offered conditional admission and required to complete the missing courses over the summer before their first semester. Courses must be college-level, for credit, and completed with a grade of B or higher; MOOC-platform courses (e.g., Coursera) do not qualify. A completed online application is required and there is no application fee. GRE/GMAT policy. For the current cycle, GRE/GMAT scores are optional but recommended as part of holistic review. Applicants must demonstrate quantitative skills in at least one of the following ways: (1) submitting GRE/GMAT results, and/or (2) completing a college-level algebra course (or a higher level of math) with a grade of B or higher. Applicants offered admission who have not demonstrated the necessary math proficiency are required to attend Heinz College's free Quantitative Skills Summer Program (QSSP), take a college-level algebra class, and/or take the GRE/GMAT as a condition of admission. The MAM program has no formal prerequisite coursework, but because the curriculum is quantitative, applicants are encouraged to complete statistics and pre-calculus/algebra coursework.

Master of Entertainment Industry Management (MEIM) — Program Overview

Key program information. Program length: 2 years, 4 semesters. Application deadlines: December 1 (Round 1), January 10 (Round 2), February 2 (Round 3). Degree earned: Master's degree. Internship: required, in addition to a practicum internship during the program's second year. Location: Pittsburgh, PA in the first year and Los Angeles, CA in the second year. The MEIM program is not STEM-designated.

Location: Pittsburgh, PA in the first year and Los Angeles, CA in the second year. The MEIM program is not STEM-designated. The Master of Entertainment Industry Management (MEIM) is a dual-city joint degree program with Carnegie Mellon University's College of Fine Arts. It combines the creative strengths of the College of Fine Arts with the management and quantitative expertise of Heinz College to prepare students for development, marketing, production, distribution, and strategy across the global entertainment industry. Students spend their first year taking core technology, analytical, and leadership classes on CMU's Pittsburgh campus (including a networking weekend in New York City), then spend their entire second year in Los Angeles completing studies while working a full-time internship in film, TV, gaming, or music. About 80% of MEIM graduates find full-time employment in the entertainment industry within three months of graduation.

A short video interview is optional but strongly recommended; in recent years more than 90% of admitted MEIM students completed a video assessment. MEIM applicants may also submit an optional one-minute video essay (uploaded to an accessible site such as an unlisted YouTube video, submitted as a URL) introducing themselves to the Admissions Committee. The video essay option is unique to the MEIM program and is not available for other Heinz programs.
